message.content: Adversaries may develop self-signed code signing certificates to lend credibility to malicious software. Code signing digitally authenticates executables and scripts, ensuring the software's integrity and origin. Users and security tools often trust signed code more than unsigned code, even if the certificate's issuer is unknown. This trust can be exploited by adversaries to bypass security measures and distribute malware. Groups like Patchwork and PROMETHIUM have used self-signed certificates from fictitious or spoofed companies to sign malicious code, enhancing its perceived legitimacy. Mitigation strategies include enforcing strict certificate validation policies, using reputation-based detection systems, and monitoring for unusual or unauthorized certificate usage. Regularly updating security tools to recognize and flag self-signed certificates can also help prevent exploitation.
